videos.title: 16 Things I Wish I Knew Before Visiting Amsterdam 🇳🇱
videos.description:
These are my top travel tips, some random facts and things I wish I had known before visiting Amsterdam 🌷

Amsterdam is one of Europe's most popular travel destinations, and I get why! I mean, where else can you find a city with more bikes than people, centuries-old buildings that lean like they've had a few too many, and canal houses with windows so big you can basically watch Dutch TV from the street? 🫣

Whether you're planning a trip to Amsterdam or you want to learn more about this very unique European city, I hope you like the video 🙌

*Where to book attraction tickets in Amsterdam* https://destinationwellknown.com/link/amsterdam-tickets

Check out my other video on *where to stay in Amsterdam* https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lw1OsQAW6vw

I hope you enjoyed this little Amsterdam video - subscribe for more travel content, and let me know what you thought about the travel tips in a comment 🤝

Looking for the best things to do in Amsterdam and Amsterdam activities that are a bit more off the beaten path? This Amsterdam travel guide includes tips, ideas & tourist traps in Amsterdam to avoid, plus insights into Amsterdam hidden gems.
tags: top things to do in amsterdam
videos.transcript: The incredible city of Amsterdam is one of those places that has a little something for everyone, whether your idea of fun is getting drunk on a boat with bottomless cheese cubes or darting from museum to museum until your sanity begs for mercy! And if you need help deciding what to do, this video will cover the best things to do in Amsterdam alongside which overhyped activities to skip because there's definitely quite a few tourist traps to avoid. Be sure to stick around until the end for a free map of all these activities and extra resources to help you plan your dream Amsterdam itinerary. In case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel tips from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe for more travel videos just like this one. And just a final money-saving tip before we begin, if you plan to do a lot of paid attractions during your time in Amsterdam it may be worthwhile to get an iAmsterdam card, which includes most major attractions for one set price but as usual be sure to crunch the numbers and see if it's worth it for you. Now, let's move on to the list. Number one: a canal cruise. If you're looking for the best most Amsterdammy thing to do during your visit, my friend, it's time to hurl yourself on a boat. Is it painfully cliched? Sure, but if there's only one touristy thing you treat yourself to in Amsterdam a canal cruise is non-negotiable. Honestly, this is the best way to admire the city and get a quick overview of its famous picturesque canals, plus for bonus fun points you could book really indulgent ones like a bottom wine and cheese cruise, which I can vouch for having done it myself recently. My tip? Go at sunset, it is absolutely dreamy. Number two: the Red Light District. Of course, I couldn't make a list of the most unique things to do in Amsterdam without including the Red Light District, so I'm going to just get it out of the way. The Red Light District is an infamous and oft-misunderstood part of Amsterdam, known the world over for legal prostitution, sex workers in windows, erotic performances, erotic shops and even a whole museum about the history of prostitution. To be honest, I don't think this area is a particularly exciting place to visit unless you're really into tourist crowds, vape shops, dodging public urination and the everpresent scent of Mary Jane or you know, actual prostitution, in which case go wild! But hey, the Red Light District is truly one of those things that most visitors feel like they need to check off their list, so of course, go indulge your curiosity but don't get your hopes up, I doubt a visit here will be the highlight of your trip. And of course, remember to remain respectful and not take any photos where you're not supposed to. Now for something a little more my speed, let's move on to number three which is to gorge on stroopwafels. If there's one treat you need to try in Amsterdam, make sure it's this one. Stroopwafels are a very thin cookie-like treat that are made by sandwiching two thin waffle wafers together with a caramelly filling in the middle. They originated in Gouda and are absolutely delicious, especially if eaten while warm. As a purist, I must implore you to try a plain one rather than one of the more aesthetic ones coated in chocolate and other toppings. A truly excellent stroopwafel will shine just fine on its own and they don't need to be fancy or expensive either. I personally have a soft spot for the big multi-packs you buy from the supermarket but don't let the foodies know I told you that. Moving on to number four, which is to stuff your face with all manner of fried snacks. Amsterdam is known as a city of vice and indulgence and if your indulgences come in the form of mysterious things deep-fried to oblivion, I have great news! Dutch food is a true wonder, from chocolate sprinkles on toast for breakfast to tiny bite-sized pancakes doused in sugar, there is no caloric limit to what these magically tall people consume. Some of the best treats though are the fried ones and honestly, if you're looking for fun things to do in Amsterdam I'd like to suggest a grand tour of the Netherlands' most prized snacks, including fries, usually served in a creamy sauce; bitterballen, which are small round deep-fried balls of a kind of beef stew that's been coated in breadcrumbs; croquettes, which are mainly a bigger version of bitterballen but shaped like a sausage and then deep fried; frikandel, which is sort of like a Dutch hot dog although they don't look super appetizing, I can vouch that they're very delicious; and kaassoufflée, think of a crumbed and deep-fried croquet only flat and it's filled with gooey cheese. Now let's move on to some of the more cultural things to do in Amsterdam, starting with number five: getting your culture on at the Rijksmuseum AKA the National Museum of the Netherlands. The Rijksmuseum is to Amsterdam as the Louvre is to Paris, it's an astounding collection of 8,000 plus pieces representing centuries of Dutch art and history, all housed in a grand building that will give you neck pains from all the inevitable oohing and aahing. But, and I say this with love, do not force yourself to go to the Rijksmuseum if you are not particularly interested in art and history, it's expensive, busy and quite a time commitment given the museum's massive size. If you only have a short time in Amsterdam, I might recommend stopping by to admire the building's exterior then focusing on some of the other activities in this video. If you do decide to go though, here's my tip: visit just before closing to avoid the crowds and don't miss the hidden library. By the way, one of my favorite Amsterdam fun facts is that there's actually a branch of the Rijksmuseum at the Amsterdam Schiphol Airport, the first museum to be opened in an airport, period, which is pretty special. And if you're more into modern art then the nearby Stedelijk Museum may be more your speed. It's dedicated to modern art and design with lots of bold and interesting installations. Located a nerdy skip away from the Rijksmuseum is another one of Amsterdam's most famous cultural must-dos: the Van Gogh Museum or, if you're pronouncing it the proper Dutch way, the "Van Gogh" Museum. This is one spot I'd recommend to anyone who is familiar with Van Gogh and is a fan of his work. After all, this is the world's largest collection of his works with hundreds of paintings, drawings and letters on display for your nosy perusal. While here, not only do you get to treat your eyeballs to some of the most famous and well-known paintings in history, they're all contextualized beautifully with tidbits about Van Gogh's life story. If you're easily moved like me or perhaps just hormonal, also like me, then this Amsterdam must-do may make you a bit emotional, so bring tissues. Onwards to number seven which is to stop by the world's largest street art museum. Straat is one of the coolest museums in Amsterdam hands down, located across the river and about a 10-minute free ferry ride from Amsterdam Central Station, in the NDSM-Werf neighborhood. Once you get there, look for the giant mural of Anne Frank and then head inside what was once a shipbuilding warehouse to see a massive museum dedicated to street art. This huge space is filled with equally large canvases, along with some quirky installation art and sculptures, including a truck hanging from the ceiling with murals on each side of it. The pieces on display here are truly breathtaking and there are more than 160 artworks by over 150 artists from all around the world. There's even a cool interactive art piece in one of the side rooms and the cafe looking out over everything is also a great spot for some refreshment. Onwards to number eight which is a big one and that is to visit a quirky museum. Listen, if I were to continue listing every single Amsterdam museum as a must-do, we would be here for weeks and I'm sure you want to get back to your loved ones in a timely manner, so let's just summarize some of the funnier weirder ones I think you might like. In Amsterdam you'll find a truly eclectic selection of museums which is why sometimes buying an iAmsterdam card can be really great because you can see some of the smaller ones and feel like it's a better deal because it's all included. For instance, in the past I visited the Pipe Museum but in addition to that, there's also the Upside Down Museum, the Katten Kabinet Museum, which is dedicated to art depicting cats and also Our House, which is an immersive museum experience dedicated to dance music, with exhibitions on the history of dance music along with installations, immersive shows and DJ-curated exhibits. Of course, besides visiting museums one of the top must-dos in Amsterdam is to simply photograph your heart out. Amsterdam is one of the most aesthetically pleasing cities in Europe with narrow crooked houses squished against each other lining picturesque canals as an endless stream of bikes glide over bridges like some kind of utopian postcard. Honestly, one of the best things to do in Amsterdam is just stare at everything and drool. I'd be happy spending days doing just that but if you're wondering where exactly to drool here are some can't miss photo spots: first, Damrak. Just outside of Amsterdam Centraal Station is an avenue known as Damrak, here is where you can get a shot of the famous dancing houses in Amsterdam that you've no doubt seen many times before, a set of crooked canal houses just behind some water where lots of canal tours depart. Close by you'll find Dam Square, which is Amsterdam's most famous square. Be sure to visit the inside of the Royal Palace if you can but otherwise beware of pigeons here, they are awful. Another beautiful photo spot is the neighborhood known as Jordaan, to the west of the city center. This area is known as one of Amsterdam's prettiest neighborhoods, where you'll find many cute cafes, restaurants, boutique shops, hotels and homes lining canals. Finally, there's the Skinny Bridge. Amsterdam is known for its many bridges crossing all its canals but the Skinny Bridge might be the most famous. This picturesque wooden bridge was featured in the 1971 James Bond film Diamonds Are Forever and at night it's lit up by tons of little light bulbs, making it very cute and romantic. Now, let's move on to number 10. Of course, it wouldn't be a list of unique Amsterdam experiences without mentioning the famous Anne Frank House, by far one of the most solemn but important places that the city contains. Anne Frank was a German-born Jewish girl who was living in Amsterdam when the Nazis invaded and subsequently spent 2 years hiding with her family in tiny concealed rooms at the place where her father had worked. She kept a diary during this period which was published after her death by her father and it is now one of the most well-known books in the world. And when you're in Amsterdam, if this piece of history interests you, you can actually visit the building and rooms where Anne spent those years of her life in hiding. This was, by far, one of the most memorable stops on my first visit to Amsterdam and now, almost a decade later, I can still remember the way it felt to walk within those same four walls where Anne penned her famous diary over 70 years ago. This is definitely a tough but important place to visit. Moving on to number 11, another must-do in Amsterdam is a bumble through De Negen Straatjes, the Nine Streets. I'm sure I butchered that name in Dutch but let's just go with it! This area is located within the Jordaan, right in the middle of the UNESCO World Heritage Site section of canals. Many of the businesses here are located in original canal houses making it infinitely charming to explore, plus some of the city's quirkier museums can be found here as well, so be sure to allocate some time to exploring this neighborhood, it's absolutely stunning. Now the next thing to do in Amsterdam, is to enjoy an amazing view. There's no shortage of places for a view in the city, which is why one of my favorite things to do in Amsterdam is simply hunt for the best one. Some that I can highly recommend including the rooftop terrace at the NEMO Science Museum, the rooftop view from the OBA library or, if you're looking for paid views, there's also the A'DAM Lookout or the DoubleTree by Hilton Sky Lounge, which is a gorgeous rooftop bar that's perfect for enjoying a cocktail and some bar snacks while watching the sunset. Now another fun thing to do in Amsterdam is visiting the world's only floating flower market. On Amsterdam Singel canal is a stretch of flower stalls that are all located on floating platforms on the the water. This flower market is the only floating market of its kind and a great spot to buy some fresh tulips, tulip bulbs or other tulip-themed souvenirs. I'll be honest, this is a very touristy spot and for the most part, you don't even realize you're standing on a floating market at all. Nonetheless, it's a nice spot to quickly browse especially if you're aggressively into tulips. Now on to number 14, which is a hidden gem of Amsterdam and that's to find some peace at the Begijnhof. Hofs or hofjes, which means courtyards, are basically little gardens surrounded by homes and the Beghijnhof is one of the most beautiful in Amsterdam and the most famous. Constructed in the Middle Ages it's actually located below modern street level, so you go through a little archway and down into a garden that's hidden from the surrounding busy streets. There are two churches located within this inner garden/courtyard area and it's a beautiful place to escape the crowds for a little moment. Now onwards to number 15, which is to snoop around a canal house. The ultraslim canal houses of Amsterdam are one of the city's signature elements but do you know what's better than gawking at them from afar? Getting to step inside one. Of course, there's a few ways to do that including staying in a canal house hotel, visiting a museum inside a canal house or booking a restaurant or bar housed inside one. Regardless of how you do it, make sure you take some time to explore one of these canal houses because there is nothing more quintessentially Amsterdam. And speaking of quintessentially Amsterdam, number 16 is to snoop around a houseboat. Another distinctive thing that makes Amsterdam Amsterdam is its multitude of houseboats. Again, there are a few ways to check out the inside of one, starting with staying in a houseboat yourself or visiting the Houseboat Museum. This is basically just a houseboat that you can go into to have a look around and while it's not too expensive, it's also very small, so I wouldn't necessarily recommend it unless you're very very nosy. Now if you're looking to get a bit more off the beaten path in Amsterdam, I can highly recommend exploring the city's Jewish Quarter. Today, there are a number of historically important preserved buildings here to explore. The Jewish Cultural Quarter is an organization that manages all the places that you can visit here and is dedicated to preserving Jewish culture in Amsterdam. One ticket will gain you access to the Portuguese Synagogue, the Jewish Historical Museum and a few other attractions in the area. This is definitely a very unique and interesting area to explore, so be sure to put it on your list. Another cool neighborhood to explore in Amsterdam is De Pjip. This is a cool neighborhood in the old south of the city which used to be a working-class area but is now a multicultural and bohemian zone brimming with cafes, restaurants, coffee shops and bars. It's also home to the famous Albert Cuyp Markt, the largest daily market in Europe, which takes place Monday to Saturday from 9:30 until 5:00, and also an excellent spot to try some fresh local treats like stroopwafels, as well as affordable groceries, clothing and more. In the complete opposite direction is another great neighborhood worth checking out: Amsterdam Noord, which is directly across the river from Amsterdam Centraal Station. Luckily, you can reach it in about 3 minutes through a free ferry and from there you can explore a bunch of attractions including the A'DAMToren (the Amsterdam Tower), the Eye Film Museum and This Is Holland. At the Amsterdam Tower, you'll find a really cool viewing platform, a thrilling swing ride, as well as a swanky revolving restaurant, cool cocktail bar, hotel, underground nightclub, and a fun burger joint. This Is Holland is a really fun 5D flight simulator experience where you get to virtually visit plenty of gorgeous places in the Netherlands through one single ticket. The Eye Film Museum is also interesting if you're interested in the history of film. A bit further up the river through a different ferry is the NDSM-Werf neighborhood. This is where the street art museum I mentioned earlier is located but there are a number of artsy attractions and hip places here to check out as well, so if you're looking for something a bit more offbeat to do in Amsterdam be sure to pay this neighborhood a visit, especially when there's cool events taking place from dance music festivals to the largest flea market in Europe. And if you're looking for a very unique and interesting place to visit in Amsterdam be sure to visit this museum whose pronunciation I have butchered too many times to attempt again! This museum is home to a fascinating secret church, one of many clandestine churches that popped up in the 17th century when Catholics weren't able to worship in public anymore. Built into the top floors of a canal house, the secret attic church is one of the most interesting secrets of Amsterdam, although these days the secret's pretty much out and there are a lot of visitors but this audio guide tour takes you through the many floors, offering fascinating insight on the history of the house and what life was like back in the day. Really, this is one of the few museums where I listened to every number on the audio guide, it's that good and I highly recommend it. The next Amsterdam must-do is to get cozy in a brown cafe. Brown cafes are informal neighborhood spots that serve beer and usually simple traditional meals, they're known as brown cafes because of their typically brown interiors, with lots of dark brown wood and dim lighting. I love visiting brown bars in the Netherlands because they're very homey, very cozy and relaxed and a great place to chill after a very long day of sightseeing. Another Amsterdam activity to add to your list is visiting the city's largest park. Amsterdam has no shortage of lovely green spaces but Vondelpark is the largest and most popular. This park was opened in 1865 and covers 120 acres of space, not far from the Museumplein. It's built with biking and walking paths, as well as wide open spaces for picnics. In summer it can get very busy here but any time of year is nice to explore the different sections and it's especially good for dog spotting. Another must-do that I feel like most guides don't tell you about is enjoying Amsterdam's Secret Garden Days. Granted, this is a very limited-time event but I happened to be in Amsterdam one year for this and I have to include it because it's so cool. If you happen to be visiting in the summer there is a 3-day period in Amsterdam known as the Open Garden Garden Days, where many of the city's secret gardens are made accessible to the public. That's right, more than 30 different canal houses open up their gardens for 3 days each June, usually the third weekend of the month and if you purchase a ticket that gives you free rein to visit all these different houses. Honestly, if you've ever wondered what the canal houses look like on the inside or what their gardens look like, this is a super cool event to attend. Lastly, it has to be said, for better or for worse Amsterdam is a city that has tons of made-for-tourist activities that most locals would sneer at as they smugly glide past on their bikes but hey, my philosophy with travel is that so long as you're enjoying it, it's not a tourist trap, so with that in mind if you're still looking for fun things to do in Amsterdam here are some final touristy Amsterdam activities you might find interesting. For instance, you can hop on a beer bike tour, you can indulge in the Heineken Experience and tour the original Heineken Brewery, or if you're traveling with kids and looking for something to do on a rainy day, there's also the touristy classics like Madame Tussauds, Ripley's Believe It or Not, and the Amsterdam Dungeon. That said, I wouldn't necessarily recommend these activities because there are way better things to be doing but if it appeals to you, now you know. Oh, and a final note, unless you're a very competent cyclist I wouldn't do a bike tour here. Amsterdam cyclists are ruthless and have no patience for lost tourists. Now all that said, I recognize that this video is barely scraping the surface. Amsterdam is an amazing city with more to do than I could ever cover in one video and tons of amazing day trips too but let me know in the comments what activities you would add and be sure to like and subscribe for more practical travel videos just like this one. I'll see you guys next week, bye!

I get it if you're unsure where to stay in Amsterdam because this city is a bit wild & weird. In this video, I travel to the capital of the Netherlands, where I check out 3 of the city's best areas ⬇️

From the charming canals of Jordaan with its boutique shops and cosy cafes to the stunning UNESCO-listed Canal District (Grachtengordel) with its historic merchant houses to the vibrant and trendy De Pijp neighbourhood where locals actually hang out - I'll show you exactly where to base yourself for the perfect Amsterdam experience 🙌

I hope this video helps you determine where to stay in Amsterdam - here are the areas I mention and the hotels I recommend:

*JORDAAN* 0:50
Once a working-class district, Jordaan is now Amsterdam's most charming area. Think narrow streets with boutique shops, cosy cafes, and traditional brown cafes (Dutch pubs). It's where locals live in beautiful canal houses and gather at neighbourhood markets. I love wandering its quiet streets early in the morning before the tourist crowds arrive.

*Best hotels in Jordaan*

*THE CANAL DISTRICT* 2:58
The Canal District is the Amsterdam you've seen in photos – concentric rings of 17th-century canals lined with elegant merchant houses and their distinctive gabled facades. While undeniably touristy, it's touristy for a good reason! This UNESCO World Heritage site is stunning to explore, especially around the "golden bend" of Herengracht. Stay here if you want that classic Amsterdam experience, and don't mind the higher prices.

De Pijp is Amsterdam's hipster haven and my favourite area for food and cool cafés. This former working-class area has transformed into a vibrant multicultural district, home to the famous Albert Cuyp Market and countless trendy restaurants and bars. videos.transcript: World famous canals, Red light district, and world-class Dutch art are in Amsterdam. But traveling to Amsterdam can be a bit... challenging. As a tour guide, I'll share all the basics you need to know for your visit to Amsterdam. I'll show you the most important tourist attractions, iconic must try dutch food, when is the best time to visit, how many days to spend, where to book a room, and the best ways to get around the city. And at the end, I'll share my favorite tips and tricks about Amsterdam. Most important tourist attraction is world famous canal district, the heart of Amsterdam with beautiful houses, best shops, bars, and restaurants. Here is also the most important museum in the country holding some of the world's biggest masterpieces. Even if you're not a fan of paintings, you've heard of famous dutch painter Vincent Van Gogh. Visiting his museum is simply a must. Another must is a deeply moving Anne Frank house. It's about a young jewish girl who was hiding here from the Nazis during the second world war. Amsterdam is also home of one of the most famous beer brands in the world, Heineken. Here are also lively squares with great architecture known for its cafes, bars, and nightlife clubs. On the outskirts of Amsterdam, you can admire famous Dutch windmills. Once the oldest industrial area in Europe has become a popular open air museum. Amsterdam is also famous for its tulips and has a unique flower market with all sorts of tulips. World famous Red light district with red lit windows also offers unique museums and cannabis coffee shops. Main square has the royal palace, beautiful gothic style church, and a popular Amsterdam's national monument. If you're brave enough, you should also visit the highest lookout point in Amsterdam, famous for its spectacular views and Europe's highest swing. This is space cake, an original Amsterdam invention that includes cannabis. Quirky food is another attraction of Amsterdam. The best place to taste popular dutch finger food is an old tram depot known as De Hallen with more than 30 food stalls from Amsterdam's most respected kitchens. Here you can try one of the best Bitterballen, the most popular Dutch street food. Bitterballen are deep fried, breadcrumbed, meat-filled balls. They come in various flavors, but the most traditional ones have a filling consisting of beef, flour, beef broth, and various seasonings. Absolutely perfect! Another popular street food is a fried fish snack called Kibbeling. Dish was born from the need to use cod leftovers as the word Kibbeling originally meant the remains. Nowadays, besides codfish different types of white fish can also be used. Kibbeling is made of battered chunks of whitefish and deep-fried. It's eaten both as a snack served in a tray directly from fishmongers, or as a main dish at the table in a restaurant. But the most interesting food in Amsterdam is a small fish from the north sea called Herring as it is eaten raw. New Herring arrives in Amsterdam every June when a traditional auction of the first barrel of fish takes place. On every corner of Amsterdam, fish stands pop up and they remain there till the end of September when the Herring season ends. Fish is chopped into bite-sized pieces and served on a paper plate with onions and pickles. If you're visiting Amsterdam in the off Herring season like me, you can still try it, but there are less places selling it and the fish is called Maatjesharing. Stamppot remains to be one of the oldest and most popular Dutch dishes. The word means mesh pot as potatoes and several different vegetables are meshed together. There are several Stamppot variations, so don't get confused by the menu as they are basically all the same thing. Stamppot can also be served with smoked sausage or cooked meat on the side. And for dessert, you should try a crispy dutch waffle. Most popular waffle is called Stroopwafel. It's like a sandwich composed of two round thin waffles that are baked crispy in a waffle iron, after which two waffles are glued together with a sweet syrup. Hmm, it's absolutely delicious. But before you start packing, let's check when is the best time to visit Amsterdam? Summer is the peak of the season due to pleasant summer temperatures and long, sunny days. But perfect weather attracts huge crowds. Popular places are packed, and hotels are full despite their premium prices. Between September and November, the crowds get thinner and the weather remains quite pleasant as accommodation options become a little bit more affordable. This is a great time to visit. Winter season is between December and March, and it can be quite unpleasant. Days are short, dark, and nearly freezing, although prices are at its lowest. Spring is nice as you can see from my footage. If you're coming to see the tulips, book your room during the last two weeks of April and the first two weeks of May. That's why I recommend a trip to Amsterdam between April and May in spring, and between September and November in autumn. And for how many nights should you book a room? Even a very long summer day is not enough to get a proper taste of Amsterdam. Sites are spread around the historical city centre and visiting museums and galleries does take some time. Besides, there are many great squares with cafes and bars where you would want to soak up the atmosphere. With a limited itinerary two nights in Amsterdam might be enough, but I would rather recommend at least three nights. If you add more you could also do some out of the town trips. But where should you stay in Amsterdam? Most attractions are clustered around historic canals of the city center. But finding a vacant room during popular months can be a bit challenging so be sure to book well ahead. On booking.com I would recommend the following neighborhoods: Oude Centrum, Jordaan, Canal belt and Museum Quarter. I would also add to the list Leidseplein neighborhood nestled on a great location between Canal belt and Museum Quarter. Oude Centrum includes the most central neighborhoods, including the famous Red light district. The area is full of historical buildings, beautiful canals, cannabis coffee shops, restaurants, stores, and all sorts of museums. Jordaan neighborhood is also known for canals and beautiful houses. Its main highlight is Anne Frank house, a museum with a heartbreaking World War two story. Canal belt includes three main canals that form a ring around the old town. They were dug during the Dutch golden age and are the most prestigious area with the most beautiful architecture. Museum Quarter is famous for museums lined around the largest square in the city. The area also has Amsterdam’s biggest and most popular city park. Even if you find your accommodation outside these neighborhoods, you should be fine as Amsterdam has decent public transport. Still the best way to see Amsterdam is on foot as the city centre is compact and easily walkable. But when you need public transport, you can choose between tram, bus or metro. For each journey it's required to check in when boarding and check out on exiting. By using a reader you will need a transport ticket or a contactless debit or credit card that also works as a ticket. Tickets come as disposable plastic smart cards and can be bought from ticket vending machines at all metro stations and most other stations. A single ticket is valid for one hour, but for more than two trips a day you should get a daily ticket. It gets activated on the first check-in and remains valid for 24 hours. Tickets can even be bought on board a tram, either from a machine or a seller in a booth, but you cannot pay cash. Part of the public transport are also ferries departing behind the central station and crossing the river. They are free of charge and don't require any tickets. But the most popular way of getting around Amsterdam is cycling. Bicycles are absolutely everywhere and there are even huge underground bicycle garages. If you want to experience the everyday commute of most residents, you will have no problems finding a bike rental shop. Most people arrive to Amsterdam via Schiphol airport. From the airport you can get to the city by train, bus or taxi. Taxis are by far the most expensive option costing between 50 to 70 euro. Trains are great, running 24 hours to multiple stations, including Amsterdam’s central station. It takes only 17 minutes and costs only 5 euro. During the day, trains run every 10 to 15 minutes and after midnight they run hourly. You can also use buses as Amsterdam airport express bus is the quickest way to places near Museumplein and Leidseplein. Before I share my favorite tips and tricks, I would like to thank you for smashing thumbs up and for your super thanks support. In Amsterdam you can safely drink tap water. The city is relatively safe and everyone speaks good english. However, you should be aware of pickpockets in the crowded parts of the city center like the Red light district. Be aware that you are not allowed to take photos of the sex workers in the Red light district. To stay out of trouble, you should keep your phone or camera in your pocket at all times. If you don't, a security will show up from nowhere and probably smash your camera. Still, if you want to take a photo of beautiful historical buildings, double check that there are no red light windows around. To save time, book ahead where possible. Otherwise expect spending a lot of time in queues and waiting for your time slot. More and more places don't accept cash so it's a good idea to have a credit or debit card. Also, be aware of bicycles and never stand or walk in the bike lane. Be sure to always take a good look both ways before crossing as silent bicycles can cause serious accidents. If you use a bicycle, be sure to always lock it with two locks. Use one for locking the front wheel to the frame and the other for locking the back wheel to a post or some other structure mounted to the ground. In Amsterdam, there is a massive difference between a cafe and a coffee shop. Cafe is a place where you drink coffee, while in a coffee shop they sell marijuana. Some of these coffee shops are only counters where you can buy weed, while others look like proper cafes intended for smoking weed. Smoking outside coffee shops might end up with a fine and the same goes for drinking alcohol outside bars. I hope this video will make your trip to Amsterdam much easier. Also check my favorite Amsterdam tours and tickets in the description below and you'll be ready to go. To learn more about the best things to do in Amsterdam, watch this video. Thanks for watching and see you next time!

videos.transcript: If you're planning a trip to Amsterdam and searching for the best places to visit, then you're at the right place because today we will be talking about the top 15 places to visit in and around Amsterdam. Not just that, we will also talk about useful tips and tricks throughout the video which will help you save money, avoid scams, and explore Amsterdam like a pro. Hey guys, welcome back to Sak Cubes and this is Saga. We spent 18 days in Europe covering eight countries, exploring hundreds of attractions, experiencing the charm of European railways, and best of all, we did everything under a tight budget. To make this video simple and easy to understand, I have divided the places into four categories. Without further ado, let's jump straight into our list of top 15 places to visit in and around Amsterdam. Imagine the Netherlands in the 18th and 19th century. This charming town near Amsterdam will transport you right to that era. Situated just 20 km from Amsterdam, Zans is an open air museum that showcases iconic Dutch traditions like windmills, wooden shoe making, a cheese factory, and traditional houses. And the best part, most of these attractions are free except for a few which we'll talk about in a bit. The moment you step into Jansashans, the first thing that you'll notice are these colorful Dutch houses, some of which look no less than a fairy tale. Many of these houses have now been converted into boutique shops or small museums. Further ahead, you will come across iconic wooden windmills, and there are a lot of them. To this date, these windmills are still operational, and you can step inside one for about €5. The windmill that we visited was used for paint production. And apparently this is the only windmill in the world still used for producing paints. From the top you get a closer look at the wings and a breathtaking panoramic view of the surroundings. However, I felt visiting inside of the windmill wasn't worth the money. On the other side of the windmill, you'll find Katarina whole cheese farm. Here you can learn about the cheesem process, snap some inorthy photos, and best of all, enjoy free cheese samples. If you're interested in buying cheese, they have an extensive collection of different varieties of cheese. Further ahead, you'll find Uden shoe workshop, which is also free to visit. Dutch wooden shoes or clogs are a famous symbol of the Netherlands, and this is the best place to witness how they are made. If you're looking for unique souvenirs, then don't miss these miniature clocks. They're adorable and look super cute. While heading back to Amsterdam, give a quick stop at the Lego city of Netherlands, Zandam. Situated between Zans and Amsterdam is this little town called Zandam, known for its stunning architecture. The first thing that catches your eyes as soon as you arrive is this iconic Intel Hotel. For a moment, you might even wonder, is it even real? This 12story masterpiece is made up of 79 wooden house facads creating a vibrant and eye-catching mosaic. Apart from taking photos here, you can take a stroll around this narrow canal surrounded by shops and it is on either side. Now tell me one thing. Do you like FL? Because no matter if your answer is yes or no, it's hard not to appreciate the next attraction to lip fields. The Netherlands is the largest producer of tulips in the world, making them an iconic part of Dutch culture and the economy. The best time to see tulips is from mid-March to midmay with the peak blooming season usually occurring in April. While Amsterdam itself doesn't have tulip farms, the most popular tulip fields are just about an hour away. The main region where tulips are grown is called Bulan Streak, also called the Dutch flour region in English. While there are many places where you can see the tulips bloom, two of the most popular spots are Kucenhof and Tulip Barn. Kukenhof is the world's largest tulip garden and a popular place among tourists. Every spring over 7 million tulips bloom here, showcasing a stunning spectrum of colors and variety. Unlike the open tulip fields, Cucenov is a landscape garden, making it convenient to see variety of flowers in one place. However, if you prefer to explore actual tulip fields, consider visiting the tulip barn. It's less crowded and you can walk right through the colorful fields. The place also features several props and photo spots, making it very Instagrammable. Now, moving from the countryside to the city, let's explore Amsterdam's famous landmarks and iconic spots. The charming canals of Amsterdam are so picture perfect that they deserve a spot on everyone's bucket list. Bridges decorated with flowers, tall gabled houses on either side, and bicycles casually parked along the railings add to the city's unique charm. These canels were constructed back in the 17th century during the Dutch Golden Age to manage water and facilitate trade. Today they are recognized as a UNESCO world heritage site and are one of the city's top tourist attractions. If you look at the map closely, you'll notice four main canals emerging out from the central station. Single Canel, Gentleman's Canel, Emperor's Canel, and Princess Canel. The farther you go from the center, the less touristy it becomes. If you want to do the most Amsterdami thing, then hop on a canal cruise ride. This 1 hour boat tour is the best way to get a quick overview of the city's canals. The best time to take the boat tour is around the sunset. That is when the city looks so dreamy. While a canal cruise remains the most popular way to explore the canals, I felt walking is even a better way to appreciate the architecture. Basically, you get time to soak in the beauty and photograph your heart out. So, do I recommend a canal cruise? H I have a mixed opinion. Most popular tourist boats are enclosed and lack immersive guided experience. If weather permits, go for an open boat. It'll be a much better experience. I have listed some of the best tour operators and the booking links along with pros and cons in my Amsterdam travel guide. This guide also includes loads of other useful informations including a detailed itinary, packing list, best places to eat, maps, free washroom locations, attraction links, opening hours, and insider tips for all the attractions. And the best part is it is mobile friendly and you can access it anytime on the go without the internet. Scan this QR code to download it or check the first link in the description box below. When exploring Amsterdam, you'll pass through Dam Square sooner or later. Dam Square is city's most famous plaza, always buzzing with crowds. This square got its name in the 13th century when a dam was built across the Amstra River, giving the city its name, Amsterl Dam, which eventually became Amsterdam. There's no dam here now, but you'll find major city landmarks like the royal palace, madame to sorts, the national monument, and the new church. As I mentioned, Amsterdam is best explored on foot, and there are many walking trails across the city. One such popular trail is between Amsterdam Central Station to Dan Square. Let's take a closer look at this route called Damra. [Music] The dam rack starts at the Amsterdam Central Station and leads you straight into the heart of the city. This stretch looks exactly how I had imagined Europe. Tramps gliding down the road, cyclist zipping by, sidewalks bustling with tourist and traditional Dutch buildings lined on both the sides just as I pictured it. Make sure to stop by and take a photo in front of the famous dancing houses of Amsterdam. I'm sure you would have seen these houses on every other photo of Amsterdam on the internet. And don't forget to try fries at Mannequin Pest. Most people miss this little passage right next to the Primark. You get beautiful photos here. Pro tip, if you need to use a washroom, head to Primark. They have a free toilet facility for customers. And if you need to refill your water bottles, there is a water fountain near the National Monument. For more such money-saving tips and tricks, check out our Amsterdam travel guide link in the description box below. Remember the four canels I mentioned earlier? On the innermost one, the single canal, you'll find Amsterdam's famous flower market known as Blowman Mart in Dutch. Apparently, it's the only floating flower market in the world. But honestly, you might not even realize the shops are actually set on a floating platform. That said, it's still a great place to browse fresh flowers, souvenirs, and tulip bulbs. If you visit in spring, you'll see a stunning variety of fresh cut tulips. A bundle of 50 tulips usually cost 8 to 15, depending on the type and the quality. If you're visiting in summers or autumn, you'll mostly find tulip bulbs instead. These bulbs, which look like small onions, are meant to be planted in autumn so they can bloom in spring. Not just tulips, you'll find a range of exotic plants, seeds, and gardening supplies. We are almost halfway into our list, and I haven't talked about food. So, let me take you to Albert Cube Market, which is a perfect place to try authentic Dutch snacks. Albert Cube Market is the largest and the most famous outdoor market in the Netherlands. It is a perfect place if you want to experience the local life, shop for souvenirs and try Dutch food. These are some of the famous food items that you can try. Stroop waffles, a traditional Dutch sweet snack made from two thin waffles with a caramel filling inside. Puffer J. These are tiny Dutch pancakes. Kibling, a tiny plate of fried fish with a sauce on the side, and Dutch herring, a traditional delicacy made from raw, lightly salted herring fish. Just a few blocks away from the Albert Cube Market. He's a Hinekin experience. The Hinekin Experience is a must visit for beer lovers or anyone looking for a fun and interactive attraction. Here you'll go through the history of Anakin, its brey process and the evolution of the brand. Towards the end you'll get complimentary beers as well. Europe is known for its beautiful parks and they look stunning especially in summers. If you're looking to relax in a lush green space right in the heart of the city then Wand Park is a place to be. It's Amsterdam's largest and the most iconic park spreading across 120 acres. Wandle Park is perfect for picnics, lazy afternoons, or just to soak in the sun. Because of its lush greenery, charming ponds, and biking trails, this park is popular among locals and tourists alike. What makes it even better is there is no entry fee to visit this park. Now, let's take a look at some of the famous neighborhoods in Amsterdam. [Music] Next on the list is strictly for 18 plus, so please skip this section if you're not over 18. The Netherlands is one of those few countries in the world where prostitution is legalized and regulated. While there are multiple brothel in Amsterdam, the most famous one is Dwallan, simply known as the red light district. It is located right in the heart of the city and it comes alive after the sunset which is indeed the best time to visit. Here you can find red glass windows where women would be standing just like a mannequin in a store display. This neighborhood is also known for selling erotic items and quirky souvenirs. If you're open to exploring something different, there is something called a peep show where you get to watch a couple in action for about €2 for€ 2 minutes. If that's not enough, there is also a live sex show with a proper theater-like setup. Not just the sex, Amsterdam is also known for its tolerance towards the consumption of cannabis based drugs like marijuana, hash, and joints. They call them soft drugs and are treated similar to alcohol and tobacco. The places that sell canabies are called coffee shops. So, if you're actually looking to have a cup of coffee, head to a cafe, not a coffee shop. Considering what Red Light District has to offer, it might feel uncomfortable for some people to visit it. And that's totally okay. You can always skip it and instead explore one of the trendiest neighborhoods in Amsterdam, the Yordan. One of the most trending neighborhoods in Amsterdam is the Yordan. It has a whole different vibes, quieter, artistic, and super charming. Originally built to accommodate the working-class people and immigrants, Yordon now is home to artists and ink professionals. This neighborhood is probably the most charming in entire Amsterdam. It offers laid-back Dutch lifestyle with boutique shops, designer stores, and trendy cafes. The famous Frank House is also situated in this locality. By the way, if you're enjoying this video so far, hit on the like button and leave a heart in the comment section below. [Music] On the other side of the I river lies another fascinating neighborhood worth exploring. Amsterdam Nod aka North Amsterdam. It's easily accessible 24 bar 7 via free ferry services from Amsterdam Central Station. In Amsterdam Nod there are mainly two areas worth exploring. The first spot is closest to the Amsterdam Central. It takes just 3 to 4 minutes to reach through the free ferry. Here you'll find Adam lookout, a popular paid attraction offering a 360°ree panoramic view of the city and Europe's highest swing on the rooftop. There is also an IIL museum for the film heads. Most people skip the other side of Amsterdam Nord known as NDSM Warf. This part of Amsterdam feels like a whole different world compared to the city center. It has a raw, rebellious and a hippie wipe. One of the highlights of this area is the Start Museum. The world's largest street art museum featuring murals and installations by international artists. Even if you don't go inside the museum, just a walk around this area is an art experience in itself. The best part of visiting NDSM is in fact the ferry ride. Unlike a short ride to Adam Lukot, the ferry to NDSM takes around 10 to 15 minutes, giving you a longer and more scenic journey across the eye river. Now, let's look at some of the most popular museums in Amsterdam. Whether you are into art or not, a visit to the largest square in Amsterdam, Museum Plane, aka Museum Square, is a must. This place is home to multiple museums, among which the Reichkes Museum and the Wango Museum are the most popular ones. The Reichkes Museum is the most visited museums in the Netherlands. It houses an astounding collection of over 8,000 pieces of Dutch art among which the Night Watch by Rembrandt is the most famous one. If you don't know, Rembrandt was one of the greatest painters in European history. His work is known for its emotional depth, primatic lighting, and incredible realism. Yet another popular museum in this area is the Wango Museum. It holds the largest collection of Vincent Wango's work. Some of his most iconic paintings include the starry night, sunflowers, the bedroom, and his self-portraits. Known for his bold colors, emotional intensity, and expressive brush strokes, Wango style was considered unconventional during the 19th century. Today, his paintings are worth millions and are considered masterpieces of modern art. If you're not into art or want to save money, you can skip both the Reichkes Museum and the Wango Museum. However, I still recommend visiting the museum complex. It's lively and full of energy. [Music] Yet another popular museum in Amsterdam beyond museum plane is the Anfrank house. Annefrank was a German-born Jewish girl living in Amsterdam. When the Nazis invaded the Netherlands, Anne's family went into hiding for 2 years. She recorded her thoughts, fear, and daily life in a diary. She eventually died in one of the concentration camps. After the war, her father found her diary and decided to publish it under the title The Diary of a Young Girl. It has since been translated into more than 70 languages and is one of the most read books in the world. This is a very place where Anne Frank and her family hid during World War II. Be aware that Anfrank walking tours on Cluke, get your guide or any other third party website do not include entry into the actual house. The only way to book the tickets to Anfrank house is through their official website and the tickets tend to sell out almost instantly. Details on how to book the tickets are shown on the screen. Take a screenshot. That was the top 15 places to visit in and around Amsterdam. If you have any questions about Amsterdam or Europe in general, feel free to ask it in the comment section below. If I missed any place that deserves to be on this list, then let me know in the comments. Check out our Amsterdam travel guide by scanning this QR code on the left or click on the right to watch Amsterdam complete guide video. See you in my next video. Until then, keep traveling.

Wondering how to spend 3 perfect days in Amsterdam? This three day Amsterdam itinerary will break down the best things to do with a short time in the city, designed to help you maximize every hour.
tags: top things to do in amsterdam
videos.transcript: If you're wondering the best way to spend three days in Amsterdam, this video is here to help. After all, I know first-hand the struggle of navigating Amsterdam's infinite sea of choices. Whether your idea of a good time is boozing on a boat with bottomless cheese or getting drunk on museum fumes instead. So, to ensure this itinerary fits your needs, no matter your interests, I've designed it in a choose your own adventure type format with activity suggestions broken up by neighborhood after five trips of sweet, sweet research. Now, in case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com, and this channel is all about practical travel advice from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing. Alright, time to get started with day one, which involves exploring the touristic core of Amsterdam and all its most famous sites. To kickstart your first day in Amsterdam, we need to make sure you're properly fueled. Because I'm going to be real with you, today's itinerary is intense. So, no matter what you grab for breakfast, make sure it's filling and fueling, preferably with a coffee or 10 to go with it. If you want to be as Dutch as humanly possible, I'd advise you simply eat some bread. Yes, the Dutch love their bread, whether topped with cheese, spreads, or chocolate sprinkles, and you really can't get more Dutch than that. If you want other options though, the good news is Amsterdam is a modern city that, like most cities, has fallen hopelessly in love with brunch spots, so there's no shortage of places to grab a more filling bite. I really enjoyed Greenwoods Singel on my last visit. Now, with your stomach properly lined, it's time for a walk. Your first morning in Amsterdam will be filled with a lot of sightseeing and canal ogling, because let's be real, there's no better way to appreciate the beauty of this city. We'll be tackling some of the city's busiest and most touristy sections first because the later we wait in the day, the busier they'll be. Crowds are inescapable in Amsterdam, but the more you can have the streets and canals to yourself, honestly, the better. Here's the walking route that I would recommend on your first morning. We're going to start at Damrak, one of the most quintessentially Amsterdam photo spots because a quick snap here is a must. From here you can proceed onwards to Dam Square via the very pretty De Beurspassage. This is Amsterdam's main square that is filled with endless pigeons but worth a quick stop. From here you can go onwards to De Negen Straatjes, The Nine Streets, a beautiful section of Amsterdam known for being packed with vintage and designer boutique stores as well as cute cafes. This is truly one of the prettiest parts in the city to stroll around and fall in love with the views. So, I'd say the best thing to do is wander around, popping into boutiques as you go, and inevitably, taking 10 million photos of canals. If you want a bit more structure to your exploration, though, there are a number of fun things you can do here, including museums if you're interested, like the Houseboat Museum, which allows you to take a nosey mossy and see what all those cool Amsterdam house boats look like on the inside. There's also others like the Museum of the Canals, which is all about Amsterdam's canal history. And Huis Marseille, the city's first photography museum, which is located inside a gorgeous 17th century canal house with a very pretty garden. Ultimately though, after all your frolics, I want you to end up at the world famous Amsterdam Bloemenmarkt, known as the world's only floating flower market. I have to say, this is an incredibly touristy place, and we're only stopping by to check it off your list. I personally wouldn't do too much shopping here, but it's fun to look at all the tulipy souvenirs and duck into the Dutch cheese shops offering liberal free samples. Now, if you don't completely fill up on samples, it's time for lunch. Today, I would recommend a quicker on-the-go lunch because there's a lot more to see. Luckily, the Dutch are master snackers, so there's no shortage of quicker options to choose from. Vlaams Friteshuis Vleminckx is a reliable pick, serving up crispy fries in an absurdly delicious sauce. Or if you want a quirky novelty experience, you can also grab something quick from a hot vending machine. Look up Febo to find one near you. Alternatively, if you'd prefer a sit-down meal, many bars will have a variety of Dutch snacks for you to try, with popular picks being an endless variety of deep fried goodies, like Dutch fries; frikandel, which is a sort of deep fried sausage; bitterballen, which are small fried balls of stew; or kroket, basically the same thing except shaped like a sausage; and kibbeling, small pieces of marinated fried fish. Do save room for some sweet treats, though. Some popular ones you can find around Amsterdam include stroopwafel, two very thin waffles sandwiched together with caramel syrup, and poffertjes, sort of like mini puffed up pancakes usually served with powdered sugar. Now with your stomach properly fueled, it's time to spend our afternoon at Museumplein. This beautiful spot is home to four major art museums, along with a big open grass area perfect for events and people watching. If you feel especially passionate about squeezing a museum in, there are plenty of excellent options right here, though I've mainly designated tomorrow to be the museum day because today is all about the vibes. If you have the time and stamina for a museum though, some very popular picks here include the Rijksmuseum, also known as the National Museum of the Netherlands, which is filled with Dutch art and history exhibitions along with a gorgeous library. There's also the Van Gogh Museum - or the Van Ghoff Museum if you want to pronounce it the Dutch way - which is, of course, dedicated to the Dutch artist Vincent Van Gogh, with the largest collection of his works in the world showing how his style evolved during his lifetime. There's also the Stedelijk Museum, which is a modern art museum that kind of looks like a bathtub from the outside and is filled with contemporary art from around the world. And the Moco Museum, a smaller modern art museum most famous for containing a number of works by famed graffiti artist Banksy. I'll be leaving links in the description, by the way, to all these attractions mentioned, so be sure to check that out for easy booking links. Now, after you've had your fill of Museumplein, it's time for the best sunset activity ever, which is a canal cruise. While it might seem touristy, you cannot visit Amsterdam and not spend some time actually on the canals. There are plenty of different canal cruises to choose from, but my personal recommendation is to make sure you go at sunset when everything is just that little bit extra magical, if the weather is cooperating. Not all Amsterdam canal cruises are created equal though, so make sure you try to get one that's at least partially open, unless you're visiting in the middle of winter. That way, you can get some nice photos. And be sure to look into some fun, unique cruise options as well, like ones offering bottomless wine and cheese. Finally, it's time for dinner, which for our first day is going to be something Dutch. To be completely honest with you, Dutch cuisine doesn't have the best reputation, not even among Dutchies, but when in Amsterdam, you should try some classics at least once. For traditional Dutch dishes, Moeders is a popular tried and true classic, offering up some homestyle eats, but there are plenty of other places serving up Dutch comfort food around the city. In any case, some dishes to have on your radar include Stamppot, which is a big sausage on a pile of potatoes mashed with other veggies and covered in gravy; Hutspot, which is a dish of boiled and mashed carrots, potatoes, and onions, often served with gravy, and sometimes with a sausage or carbonade; and Erwtensoep, traditional Dutch split pea soup, usually so thick your spoon can stand up in it, often including chunks of pork and other veggies as well. And there are plenty more comfort dishes where those came from. Of course, if you're not feeling the Dutch cuisine, then there are plenty of other options. Just make sure you feed yourself well because our evening's not quite finished yet. After dinner, it's time to see a whole other side of Amsterdam. Again, here you have a choice. If you want to see Amsterdam's infamous Red Light District, evening is the time to do it when the notorious red-lit windows are in full display and where you'll be able to take your pick from the neighborhood's many unique shows, shops, and bars. But while the Red Light District is a big part of Amsterdam's tourist allure, and I get it if you want to have a look out of curiosity, I don't personally find it to be the best place to hang out as a tourist. If you're looking for something cozier, I can definitely recommend visiting a brown café or brown bar instead. These bars are public establishments and are great spots to have a drink and some nibbles and maybe even strike up a conversation with a friendly local. My recommendation is In 't Apjen, right by the Red Light District. It's reportedly the oldest bar in Amsterdam and filled with monkey statues, allegedly because sailors used to pay for drinks with monkeys back in the day. Now, after a fun evening, it's time to get some sleep and wake up bright and early for day two, which is our big history and culture day. After caffeinating sufficiently over breakfast, it's time to tackle a large Amsterdam attraction of your choice before the afternoon crowds roll in. My personal recommendation would be to visit the Anne Frank House, the very building where Anne Frank and her family hid during the Nazi occupation of the Netherlands, and also where she penned her famous diary, which would later be published as Diary of a Young Girl. With a spot as popular as this, honestly, you'll be taking whatever time slot you can get, but if you can, aim to grab a ticket for the very first time slot of the day. With tickets sold out every single day, going first thing is your only bet at seeing the space somewhat empty. If you aren't able to secure tickets in advance, or if you'd rather do something else, then I'd recommend picking another big Amsterdam attraction like the Rijksmuseum or the Van Gogh Museum, as these tend to be the least busy if you're able to nab the first entry slot. Of course, there are plenty of other attractions and activities to choose from, so be sure to check out my full guide on things to do in Amsterdam for more ideas. Now, after some shameless sightseeing, it's time for lunch. And for that, I'd recommend a food tour. This is a great option in Amsterdam because many of the typical must-tries are ones that newbie travelers may not want to eat a giant portion of, like fried goods of all kinds or herring. So, take your pick from one of the many organized food tours in the city or simply DIY your own by trying some of Amsterdam's most popular street foods. Once you are too full to walk, it's time to tackle another shamelessly touristy activity or perhaps another Amsterdam neighborhood. You could, for instance, head back to Jordaan, where the Anne Frank House is, to explore more of its quintessentially Amsterdam views and many museums, from quirky ones like the Pianola Museum, which is dedicated to self-playing pianos, to more touristy spots like the Amsterdam Cheese Museum or Amsterdam Tulip Museum. Or if you're open to going a little farther afield, the Jewish Quarter has a number of interesting attractions like the Joods Museum, all about the history of Jews in Amsterdam; the Portuguese Synagogue of Amsterdam, which is a grand synagogue constructed in 1675; and the National Holocaust Museum, a sobering but important museum about how the Holocaust impacted the Netherlands. Amsterdam-Noord offers a few more options as well, but if you're following this itinerary closely, we'll already be headed that way tomorrow. In any case, once you've had your fill of exploring, it's time for dinner. Since last night you tried some classic Dutch cuisine, tonight let's mix things up with an Indonesian rijsttafel, a popular offering with roots in the Dutch colonization of Indonesia. Literally meaning rice table, a rijsttafel offers a wide variety of small Indonesian dishes served with rice and sides, allowing diners to sample many different flavors in one meal. Nowadays, there are lots of great restaurants in Amsterdam where you can sample one. I really enjoyed mine at restaurant Long Pura in Jordaan. And if you're not too stuffed with food to move after dinner, I suggest trying out another Dutch specialty before bed, Jenever, a traditional spirit from the Netherlands, considered the ancestor of modern gin, made from distilled malt wine and flavored with juniper berries. Locals have been drinking it for centuries, usually served in a small tulip-shaped glass filled to the brim. You can probably get some Jenever in any old bar in the city, but if you want to take it up a notch, you can book a specific experience or head to a distillery. Don't get too carried away though, because for day three, we have big plans. On your last day, it's time to get away from the central core of Amsterdam and see more of what the city has to offer. This will very much be a build your own adventure type day, so feel free to swap out my morning or afternoon recommendations. Alternatively, if you feel like you've explored enough of Amsterdam, you can spend your third day enjoying one of Amsterdam's many amazing day trips. Some of the most popular options include Zaanse Schans, a very cutesy neighborhood not too far from Amsterdam, known for its traditional green wooden buildings with seven different museums and eight historic windmills. You can also opt to visit another Dutch city while you're here. Within an hour of Amsterdam Central Station, you can easily visit Rotterdam with its unique modern architecture; Utrecht, which feels a lot like Amsterdam with a fraction of the crowds; Delft, famed for its iconic blue pottery and beautiful canals; and The Hague, home to the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court. All that said, if you want to stay in Amsterdam for your final day, here's how I would spend it. After grabbing breakfast, it's time to spend our morning exploring a new side to Amsterdam. Across the water from Amsterdam Central Station, you have the gloriously cool Amsterdam-Noord neighborhood. Depending on your interests, you have two main options for this morning's Noord adventure. Option A is NDSM-Werf. This section is a little further away, about a 10-minute free ferry ride from Amsterdam Central. Once a ship building yard, today the many old warehouses have been converted into quirky art spaces and cool spots to eat. A highlight here is the Straat Museum, dedicated to massive pieces of street art, but you'll also spot plenty of street art in the outside areas too. Make sure you stop off for a drink at Pllek, a restaurant made from shipping containers with an artificial beach overlooking the river. Now, option B is hopping over to This is Holland and its attractions. Directly across the river from Amsterdam Central and only about a 3 minute free ferry ride is a section of the Noord neighborhood home to some very cool attractions including the Eye Film Museum, A'dam Toren, and This Is Holland, which is a 5D flight simulator which takes you on a virtual tour of the whole country. Of course, this is quite touristy, but I've heard really good things. Alternatively, you can explore further inland and find some cool off the beaten path parks, attractions, and cafes. In any case, pick the option that works for you, or maybe do both in one morning if you're ambitious. But after that, it's time for lunch. If you haven't tried Dutch pancakes or apple pie at this point in your trip, now is the time. Dutch pancakes are commonly eaten at lunch or dinner rather than breakfast. So, you have the country's blessing. As for apple pie, this is known as a filling anytime kind of treat. And once you see the portion sizes that they do here, you'll see why I didn't allocate any extra time or room for other foods. There are plenty of places around the city to secure these sweet treats. If you're heading back to the other side of the water, some very popular places include Het Papeneiland, a historic café which contains a secret passageway and vies for the title of best apple pie in the city alongside Winkel 43, another popular spot that is guaranteed to have a line-up. Now, take your sugar rush and press onwards for your afternoon activity: a niche nerdy thing that you are interested in. Amsterdam is filled with museums and attractions. You could truly not do them all in a year, let alone 3 days. So, for our final afternoon in the city, it's time to prioritize. Let's do something that you are personally very interested in. The amazing thing about Amsterdam's museum selection is that you'll find something no matter how niche your interest. From cats and pipes to houseboats, science, maritime history, and more. If you want to do something more unique to Amsterdam, I recommend heading back towards the Red Light District and seeing the museum Ons' Lieve Heer op Solder, Our Lord in the Attic, a chapel hidden away in the attic of a 17th century house, which is a relic from back when it was forbidden to practice one's Catholic faith in public. Amsterdam's oldest building, the Oude Kerk, is also close by, along with some museums related to the Red Light District itself. That said, art lovers are also spoiled for choice. There's the Rembrandt House where you can learn about Rembrandt's life and works in a building that he once lived in. Or Museum Van Loon, a 17th century canal house that's filled with beautiful paintings, porcelain, and furniture as well as an impressive garden. Or for something completely different, science nerds might like the NEMO Science Museum, full of interactive science exhibits. Other options include Bodyworlds and Micropia, the world's first museum that focuses on the fascinating world of microorganisms. Or there are plenty of quirkier options too, like De Kattenkabinet, a small museum in a historic canal house that's packed with art focusing on cats. There's even a museum fully dedicated to pipes, aptly named the Amsterdam Pipe Museum. Of course, if after all that you just want to chill, a trip out to Vondelpark or one of the city's many other sprawling green spaces might just be what you need. Now, after your final touristy frolic, it's time to move on to dinner, which should be something memorable. I know that's vague, but hear me out. By now, you should have already ticked off all the major must-dos food-wise off your list, so the focus of your final meal should really just be on something unique and special that you'll remember for the rest of your life. Booking a beautiful canal side restaurant, for instance, will guarantee that you end the trip on a high note. Or you could go for a unique experience dinner, like a three- course dinner in the former first class waiting rooms at Amsterdam Central Station, or maybe even a canal dinner cruise. On a budget, a picnic with a view can be equally unforgettable with the NEMO Science Museum rooftop and Museumplein being some nice options. Alright, I hope that was helpful. Be sure to like and subscribe for more travel videos just like this one and I'll see you next time. Bye!
